stories.headline: Governor Polis declares disaster emergency as wildfires spread across Western Colorado
stories.summary:
Governor Jared Polis declared a disaster emergency on June 27, authorizing the Colorado National Guard to support wildfire response efforts as multiple fires burn across the state's Western Slope amid extreme heat, gusty winds, and critically low humidity. The National Preparedness Level has reached 3, indicating significant wildland fire activity nationwide and strained shared resources.

The largest active fire, the merged Snyder Mesa/Jones Fire, has burned approximately 28,264 acres after two fires combined on Saturday evening. Originating in Utah, the blaze crossed the state line into Mesa County, where the Mesa County Sheriff's Office has issued pre-evacuation notices for areas of western Mesa County and deputies have conducted door-to-door notifications.

The Gold Mountain Fire, burning north of Ouray near the Bachelor Syracuse Mine Tour area, prompted a mandatory evacuation order for residents in the Peck's Trailer Park and Red Stone Road areas. Highway 550 between Ouray and Whispering Pines has been closed to accommodate firefighting operations, with an evacuation center established at Ridgway Secondary School.

The Red Rock Fire began Saturday morning near Redrock Canyon, roughly 12 miles northeast of Grand Junction, and has grown to approximately 33 acres on Bureau of Land Management lands. Crews are utilizing aircraft and engine support for suppression. The Dry Creek Fire in Garfield County remains at 316 acres with 90% containment, and evacuation orders for the Grass Mesa area have been lifted.

stories.headline: Arapahoe County deputies fatally shoot man who struck deputy with metal pole in Centennial backyard
stories.summary:
Arapahoe County sheriff's deputies fatally shot a man in the backyard of a home in the 4700 block of East Caley Place in Centennial early Saturday morning after the man struck a deputy with a large metal pole. Deputies had responded at 6:09 a.m. to a 911 call from a resident who reported seeing a person acting suspiciously in a neighboring backyard.

Deputies encountered the man carrying the pole, who moved toward them and struck one deputy. Less-lethal force including a Taser was attempted but proved ineffective, and two deputies subsequently discharged their firearms. Deputies administered medical aid before paramedics arrived, but the man was pronounced dead at the scene. The deputy who was struck sustained minor injuries.

Both deputies have been placed on paid administrative leave pending the outcome of the investigation, which is being conducted by the 18th Judicial District. The Arapahoe County coroner's office will release the name of the deceased man. The sheriff's office said the investigation remains active as evidence collection continues.

stories.headline: Progressive leaders criticize congressional candidate Melat Kiros for refusing to call Boulder firebombing antisemitic
stories.summary:
Three progressive Colorado leaders have publicly criticized Melat Kiros, the democratic socialist challenging U.S. Rep. Diana DeGette in the June 30 Democratic primary for the 1st Congressional District, for declining in a recent interview to characterize last year's deadly firebombing attack on a Jewish community rally in Boulder as antisemitic. Kiros said in the 9News interview that she did not know what was in the perpetrator's heart, drawing sharp rebukes days before voters go to the polls.

Mohamed Sabry Soliman was convicted and sentenced to life in prison for the June 1, 2025, attack, during which he used a makeshift flamethrower and hurled homemade bombs at a group gathered to raise awareness of Hamas hostages, yelling "Free Palestine!" as he did so. Karen Diamond, 82, died from injuries sustained in the attack. The demonstrators were predominantly Jewish.

State Sen. Julie Gonzales, state Rep. Yara Zokaie — who has endorsed Kiros — and attorney general candidate David Seligman each told the Colorado Sun that the attack was clearly antisemitic. Zokaie said the answer was obvious, while Gonzales said Kiros missed an opportunity for community healing given that a person died and multiple families were devastated.

Kiros has built her campaign largely around criticism of Israel, and polls suggest she may be on the verge of unseating DeGette amid broader discontent with Democratic incumbents. The primary election is June 30, with ballots due by 7 p.m.

stories.headline: Six candidates compete for Colorado attorney general as June 30 primary approaches
stories.summary:
Six candidates — four Democrats and two Republicans — are vying to become Colorado's next attorney general ahead of the June 30 primary election. Current AG Phil Weiser vacated the race to run for governor in the Democratic primary, opening the seat to a crowded field. Unaffiliated voters may participate in either party's primary but not both.

On the Democratic side, Secretary of State Jena Griswold is among those seeking the nomination. Griswold cited her seven-plus years managing statewide elections, including accountability proceedings against former Mesa County Clerk Tina Peters, as preparation for the role. She identified reproductive rights protection, immigration enforcement accountability, gun violence prevention, and fighting corporate pollution as key priorities, and pledged to continue legal challenges against the Trump administration.

Other Democratic candidates include David Seligman, who has been visible in primary-season debates and was recently quoted criticizing congressional candidate Melat Kiros over her Boulder firebombing comments. Two Republican candidates are also on the ballot, arguing the state's top law enforcement office should change direction. Ballots must be received by 7 p.m. on June 30.

stories.headline: Nuggets trade down at NBA draft, select Trevon Brazile at No. 35; columnist questions front office strategy
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets passed on using the 26th pick in the NBA Draft, instead trading it to the San Antonio Spurs for a pair of second-round selections. The Spurs used the pick on Tarris Reed Jr., a physical big man whom Denver Post columnist Troy Renck noted will now be available to guard Nikola Jokic as a member of a conference rival the Nuggets are trying to chase in the standings.

With one of the acquired second-round picks, the Nuggets selected forward Trevon Brazile at No. 35. Co-general manager Jon Wallace said it was important the team avoid "draft fever" and described the approach as disciplined. Brazile and fellow second-round pick Bryce Hopkins are expected to receive non-guaranteed contracts, with both players likely headed to the G League.

Renck's column drew a wider comparison between the Nuggets' conservative offseason approach and the Colorado Avalanche's aggressive moves under GM Joe Sakic, who has executed three trades this offseason while creating cap space and still retaining flexibility. The column raised questions about whether Kroenke Sports & Entertainment president Josh Kroenke's close relationships with Nuggets players may be influencing the team's reluctance to make bold moves, or whether second-apron financial penalties are the primary driver of the cautious strategy.

stories.headline: Colorado native Wyndham Clark wins second U.S. Open title at Shinnecock Hills
stories.summary:
Wyndham Clark, a Denver native, claimed his second U.S. Open championship at Shinnecock Hills Golf Club, adding to the title he first won in 2023 at Los Angeles Country Club. Clark's victory marks a significant achievement for Colorado golf and cements his standing among the top players in professional golf.

Clark grew up in the Denver area before pursuing a professional career on the PGA Tour. His first U.S. Open win made him one of the most prominent Colorado-connected golfers in recent memory, and this second major title reinforces that status. The U.S. Open is considered one of the four major championships in professional golf.

stories.headline: Avalanche trade Jack Drury to Nashville Predators, acquire Svechkov and L'Heureux
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche have traded center Jack Drury to the Nashville Predators in exchange for forwards Fedor Svechkov and Lleyton L'Heureux, continuing an active offseason under general manager Joe Sakic. The deal represents the third trade Sakic has executed this offseason as the Avalanche reshape their roster in pursuit of another Stanley Cup run.

Svechkov, a 22-year-old forward, was Nashville's first-round pick in 2021 and brings offensive upside to Colorado. L'Heureux is a physical forward prospect. The trade follows earlier deals that sent Ross Colton to Nashville and Valeri Nichushkin to Columbus, moves that combined have generated five draft picks and three players while clearing significant salary cap space for the Avalanche.

stories.headline: Denver-based Birdcall chicken sandwich chain plans expansion to up to 85 Midwest locations by 2031
stories.summary:
Denver-based fast-casual restaurant chain Birdcall is planning a significant Midwest expansion, with plans to open up to 85 new locations by 2031. The chicken sandwich concept, which has built a following in the Denver metro area, is targeting markets across the Midwest as it scales beyond its Colorado roots.

The expansion represents one of the more ambitious growth plans announced by a Denver-born restaurant brand in recent years. Birdcall has positioned itself in the competitive fast-casual chicken segment, which has seen strong consumer demand nationally. Details on specific target markets and the financing structure for the expansion were not immediately available.

stories.headline: Avenues at Northfield launches multimillion-dollar redevelopment with pedestrian mall and Wayfair anchor
stories.summary:
The Avenues at Northfield shopping center in northeast Denver has launched a multimillion-dollar redevelopment project that will include a pedestrian mall and online home goods retailer Wayfair as an anchor tenant. The project marks a significant reinvestment in the Northfield area, which has been one of Denver's larger retail corridors.

The inclusion of Wayfair, which has been expanding its physical retail presence nationally, signals a broader shift in how large shopping centers are reconfiguring themselves to attract e-commerce brands seeking a brick-and-mortar footprint. The pedestrian mall component is part of a broader effort to make the development more walkable and experience-oriented. Additional details on the timeline and total investment were not immediately disclosed.

stories.headline: Iconic Piney River Ranch near Vail to close after Denver Water lease negotiations fail
stories.summary:
Piney River Ranch, a beloved 40-acre resort, wedding venue, and outdoor recreation destination along Piney Lake near Vail, will end operations this season after its operator failed to reach a new lease agreement with Denver Water, which owns the land. General Manager Monique Busold announced the closure after two and a half years of unsuccessful negotiations to extend the lease she first signed in 2012.

The current lease expires March 1, 2027, but since the property has no winter operations, this summer season — running through September 26 — will be the final one under Busold's management. The ranch, located approximately 13 miles north of Vail at the end of Red Sandstone Road, offers canoeing, fishing, cabin rentals, camping, and other outdoor experiences that have made it a fixture for Vail-area tourists and locals for over a decade.

Busold expressed gratitude to the Vail community and visitors from around the world who have supported the ranch over the years. Denver Water's plans for the property following the lease expiration have not been announced. The closure will leave a significant gap in the Vail Valley's roster of family-friendly outdoor destinations.

stories.headline: Centennial loses 18 acres to Aurora as Regis Jesuit High School seeks to unify campus under one city
stories.summary:
An 18-acre parcel at the northwest corner of East Arapahoe Road and South Olathe Street in Centennial will transfer to Arapahoe County control in mid-July and then be annexed into Aurora, after the Centennial City Council approved a rarely used municipal disconnection process on June 16. The move was initiated by Regis Jesuit High School, which owns the property and wants its entire campus consolidated within Aurora's jurisdiction.

The school, which enrolls approximately 1,600 students and has operated in Aurora since opening its current campus 36 years ago, purchased the Centennial parcel in 2017 for athletic fields, a fieldhouse, a retreat center, and parking. Having portions of the campus in two separate cities created complications involving utility billing, land-use regulations, and emergency response routing, according to the school's board of trustees vice chair Diana Rael.

Centennial Councilman Don Sheehan, whose district shrinks as a result, initially opposed the move but said the positives ultimately outweigh the negatives. The disconnection and subsequent annexation is unusual for the Denver suburbs, where municipalities have generally been on an expansion trajectory. Aurora, Colorado's third-largest city, will gain just over 18 acres from the transaction.

stories.headline: Green gentian plants superbloom across Colorado mountain meadows, reaching up to 5 feet tall
stories.summary:
A rare superbloom of green gentian, also known as monument plant or frasera speciosa, is currently underway across Colorado's alpine meadows, with flower-covered stalks reaching 5 feet or more in height. The phenomenon, called a masting event, occurs when large numbers of the plants bloom simultaneously — something that happens only a few times each decade. The last comparable event was in 2019.

Scientists believe the current bloom was triggered by the unusually wet monsoon season of summer 2022. Because green gentians pre-form their flower stalks underground, the plants require approximately four years between the environmental trigger and visible blooming. Rocky Mountain Biological Laboratory researcher David Inouye, who has studied the plant since 1973, surveyed the area near Gothic on Friday and counted roughly 3,000 flowering plants standing 5 to 6 feet tall.

Superblooms are currently being reported from Summit County through the San Juan Mountains, with confirmed sightings near Crested Butte, Gothic, Gunnison, Telluride, Silverton, and Durango. Each individual green gentian plant lives between 20 and 80 years and blooms only once before dying. Biologist Marija Helt said the spectacle is expected to last approximately two more weeks, making it a notable attraction for Coloradans heading into the high country.

weather.notable: About 13 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I do all my own stunts. This morning I reached the top shelf without a step stool. Barely survived.

topStory.headline: Trump Nominates Oklahoma Law Enforcement Veteran Lance Schroyer to Lead ICE
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump announced Saturday the nomination of Lance Schroyer to serve as director of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, tapping the Oklahoma law enforcement veteran to permanently lead the agency at the center of his administration's sweeping deportation campaign. Trump made the announcement via Truth Social, calling Schroyer a patriot with decades of operational experience and praising his record locking up what the president described as "the worst of the worst."

Schroyer brings more than 29 years of law enforcement experience to the nomination, including service as a U.S. Marine and a career with the Oklahoma Department of Public Safety, where he reached the rank of major over the Emergency Services Unit. He currently serves as senior advisor to Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin, a former U.S. senator from Oklahoma. Mullin endorsed the pick publicly, noting that Schroyer had run large-scale operations and worked alongside state and federal partners to remove undocumented immigrants from Oklahoma under the 287g program, a federal initiative that partners local and state law enforcement agencies with ICE.

The Department of Homeland Security described Schroyer as a specialist in interagency collaboration, complex tactical planning, and constitutional safeguards in policy implementation. Both Trump and Mullin called on the Senate to move quickly to confirm the nomination, underscoring the administration's emphasis on accelerating its immigration enforcement agenda.

The nomination highlights a prolonged leadership vacuum at ICE, which has operated without a Senate-confirmed director for nearly a decade. The last confirmed director left the post in early 2017, at the close of the Obama administration, leaving the agency reliant on a succession of roughly a dozen acting directors. The most recent acting director, Todd Lyons, who oversaw the administration's deportation crackdown for more than a year, announced his departure in April; David Venturella has served as interim chief since June 1.

If confirmed, Schroyer would take the helm of an agency that has been at the forefront of one of the most aggressive immigration enforcement efforts in recent American history. His confirmation hearing, should it be scheduled, is expected to draw significant scrutiny from both supporters and critics of the administration's immigration policies.
